rationale_for_baseline_tier: A bilateral cold-triggered triphasic Raynaud's attack recovering with warming, with strong pulses, no persistently white/numb or black digit, and no cold pulseless limb, is a UCTD/Raynaud's flare — not critical ischaemia or embolism — and is managed with rewarming, cold avoidance, simple analgesia and prompt GP/rheumatology review (T2), with a clear critical-ischaemia safety-net. T2 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 (ED) for a single digit staying white/numb >2h despite warming, a black/necrotic tip, or a cold pulseless limb.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single finger staying completely white and numb for over 2 hours despite warming, a finger tip turning dark blue/black, or one hand/limb becoming persistently cold, pale and pulseless
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates critical/irreversible digital ischaemia or arterial embolism — an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any finger stays completely white and numb for more than 2 hours despite warming, a finger tip turns dark blue/black, or a hand/limb becomes persistently cold, pale and pulseless — go to ED or call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to warm up or use hands to manage food/medication while isolated
escalation_edges.rationale: A cold home and impaired hand function perpetuate attacks and impair self-care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't warm up or manage food/medication because of your hands, arrange prompt help — a friend/family or a home service.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone in a cold house with impaired hand function
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Address home warming and practical support; enable the in-person review.
Arrange rheumatology review and cold-avoidance/CCB management; monitor for CTD progression.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Cold has set off a Raynaud's attack from your UCTD — your fingers go white, blue, then red and painful as blood returns. Rewarm gently in warm (not hot) water, warm your home, wear gloves, and take simple painkillers for your joints. Because both hands are recovering and no finger is black, this isn't a blocked artery, but arrange a GP/rheumatology review for a blood-vessel medicine.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Rewarm now and arrange in-person GP/rheumatology review within a week; use ED/000 for the critical features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Go to ED for a persistently white/numb or black digit, or a cold pulseless limb; otherwise manage with warming and arrange review.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the bilateral triphasic Raynaud's flare with mild synovitis on UCTD, recovering perfusion, and the need for CCB/DMARD consideration and CTD monitoring.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Self-care advice with no critical-ischaemia/embolism screen, missing a threatened digit or a cold pulseless limb.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A critically ischaemic digit or an embolic limb is missed and tissue is lost.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for a recovering Raynaud's attack, over-medicalising a manageable flare.
